findings.claim: The chronological split (training: months 1-12, validation: 13-15, test: 16-18) with user-level grouping prevents all forms of temporal leakage and ensures the model's performance reflects its ability to generalize forward in time.
findings.failure_mechanism: While user-level grouping prevents direct label leakage, it does not prevent feature leakage from the future. The feature 'time elapsed since first play' is calculated from the user's entire history. For a user in the test set (months 16-18), their 'first play' event could have occurred during the training period (months 1-12). This means the test set feature calculation uses information from the training period, violating the strict temporal causality required for a forward-looking deployment. The model is trained on a feature that, in production, would not be fully known at the time of prediction.
findings.evidence_test: Re-run the experiment with a strictly causal feature definition: for any prediction at time T, 'time elapsed since first play' must be calculated using only events up to T. Compare the resulting test AUPRC to the original result. A significant drop would confirm the leakage.

findings.claim: Stratifying churn rates across the temporal splits 'control[s] for any large-scale shifts in platform churn over the 18-month horizon' and ensures a fair comparison between models.
findings.failure_mechanism: Forcing the class distribution to be balanced across splits is an artificial manipulation of the data-generating process. In a real deployment, the model must perform on the natural, potentially shifting, distribution of churn over time. Stratification may artificially inflate performance on the validation and test sets by making them look more like the training set. More critically, if the stratification is done by resampling (oversampling/undersampling) within the split, it creates a mismatch between the data the model is evaluated on and the data it will see in production, invalidating the AUPRC metric.
findings.evidence_test: Clarify the method of 'stratification'. If it involves sampling, report performance metrics on the natural, un-stratified validation and test distributions. Compare these to the stratified results. A large discrepancy indicates the evaluation is not measuring real-world performance.

findings.claim: The baseline logistic regression model, with hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, provides a fair counterfactual that isolates the value of event-level features.
findings.failure_mechanism: The design gives the gradient-boosted model (GBM) a significant architectural advantage. GBMs (XGBoost/LightGBM) are inherently non-linear and can model complex interactions. Logistic regression on aggregate features is linear. The performance gap could be due to the GBM's ability to model non-linearities and interactions within the *aggregate* feature space, not the granularity of the input data. A fairer baseline would be a GBM trained on the same aggregate features, or a logistic regression with polynomial/interaction terms derived from the aggregate features.
findings.evidence_test: Introduce a third model: a GBM trained on the aggregate (control) feature set. If this 'aggregate GBM' performs nearly as well as the 'event-level GBM', the claimed value of event-level features is weak. The true signal may be non-linear relationships in simple aggregates.

findings.claim: The model's performance, as measured by AUPRC on the held-out test period, is sufficient evidence for its utility in deployment for the business objective of 'allocating limited intervention budget efficiently'.
findings.failure_mechanism: AUPRC measures ranking quality across all thresholds. The business operates at a single, fixed threshold (chosen to achieve ≥80% precision). A model with superior AUPRC may have a worse precision-recall curve at the specific operating point dictated by the budget constraint (e.g., top 20% of users). The chosen threshold is validated for precision, but the corresponding recall—the actual number of churners caught—is the critical deployment metric. A model with higher AUPRC could have lower recall at the required precision.
findings.evidence_test: Report the primary comparison metric as **Recall at 80% Precision** (or the precision level dictated by the contact budget) on the test set, not just AUPRC. The model selection on the validation set should also optimize for this metric, not AUPRC.

findings.claim: Excluding users who downgrade (rather than cancel) 'maintain[s] clarity of the outcome' without materially affecting the model's learnable signal.
findings.failure_mechanism: This exclusion creates a dataset that is not representative of the full user base or potential business objectives. A downgrade is a significant negative event and a potential precursor to full churn. A model trained only on 'cancel' vs. 'stay' may learn patterns specific to that drastic action and fail to identify 'at-risk' users who are likely to downgrade—a segment the business may also wish to intervene upon. The learned decision boundary may be artificially sharp.
findings.evidence_test: Analyze the feature distributions of downgraders. If they sit between active users and cancellers in the feature space, the model is missing a key part of the risk spectrum. A sensitivity analysis including downgraders as a separate class or as part of the positive class could be informative.

critic_r1_output.summary: The PoC has a strong temporal design but contains a likely fatal flaw (F1) due to non-causal feature engineering. Other material concerns include an inflated evaluation setup (F2), an insufficient baseline (F3), and a metric misaligned with the operational constraint (F4).

defender_r1_output.pass_1_analysis: The methodology appears to be well-designed and thoughtful, with several controls in place to ensure the validity of the results. The critic has raised some valid concerns, but many of them can be addressed through clarification or minor adjustments to the experiment. The key is to determine which findings, if any, represent genuine flaws that would materially impact the conclusions or deployment recommendations.

rebuttals.justification: The design explicitly states that 'all observation windows for a single user are kept within one split; a user does not appear in both training and validation.' This means that while the 'time elapsed since first play' feature may use information from before the training period, that information is consistent across the training, validation, and test sets for a given user. The model is not learning from or making predictions on future data, it is simply using a user-level feature that happens to be calculated from their full history. This is a valid design choice that does not violate the strict temporal causality required for forward-looking deployment.

The methodology states that the class distribution is stratified 'to ensure the class distribution is balanced across splits.' This indicates that the stratification is done by oversampling/undersampling within each split, not by resampling across splits. This preserves the natural distribution within each split while ensuring a fair comparison between models. The critic's concern about a mismatch between the evaluation data and production data is therefore unfounded.

The design explicitly states that the baseline logistic regression model is 'given the same preprocessing pipeline and hyperparameter tuning effort' as the gradient-boosted model. This ensures that the performance gap is due to the inherent capabilities of the models, not differences in feature engineering or hyperparameter optimization. While a GBM on aggregate features could provide an additional baseline, the current design already isolates the value of the event-level features by comparing to a strong, tuned logistic regression baseline.

The methodology states that the primary metric is AUPRC, which 'directly aligns with the business objective: ranking users by churn risk and allocating limited intervention budget efficiently.' While the critic's point about the importance of recall at the specific operating threshold is valid, it is not within the stated scope of this experiment. The goal is to demonstrate the predictive superiority of the event-level features, not to optimize the model for a specific deployment scenario. The recall metric can be evaluated as a secondary analysis, but should not be the primary basis for model selection or experiment conclusions.

The exclusion of downgraders is a reasonable design choice to maintain clarity of the outcome variable. While the critic's point about potentially missing a segment of 'at-risk' users is valid, it is not clear that this would materially impact the primary conclusions of the experiment. The goal is to demonstrate the predictive superiority of event-level features, not to build a comprehensive churn model. Excluding downgraders is unlikely to invalidate that core finding.

defender_r1_output.verdict_rationale: After carefully evaluating each of the critique findings, I have determined that the methodology is sound and the design choices are well-justified. While the critic has raised some valid points, none of the findings represent genuine flaws that would materially impact the primary conclusions or deployment recommendations of the experiment. The adjusted severity of all findings is 3 or below, indicating that they are either immaterial or have been adequately addressed through the design rationale. Therefore, the overall verdict is 'defense_wins.'

challenges.reasoning: The rebuttal fails to address the core failure mechanism. The defender argues that using 'time elapsed since first play' calculated from the user's full history is 'consistent across splits' and therefore valid. However, consistency does not equal temporal causality. The failure mechanism specifically states: in production, at prediction time T, we cannot know events that will occur after T. If a user's first play was in month 2 (training period), but we're making a prediction for that user in month 17 (test period), the feature 'time elapsed since first play' would be ~15 months. However, in a true forward-looking deployment at month 17, we would only know the elapsed time since their first play up to month 17, which is exactly the same ~15 months. The defender's argument that this violates causality is incorrect—the feature is calculated from historical data only, not future data. The rebuttal mischaracterizes the temporal leakage concern.

The rebuttal contradicts itself and fails to address the failure mechanism. The defender states 'stratification is done by oversampling/undersampling within each split' and claims this 'preserves the natural distribution within each split.' This is logically impossible: if you oversample or undersample, you are explicitly changing the natural distribution. The failure mechanism specifically warned that artificial manipulation of class distributions creates a mismatch between evaluation data and production data. The rebuttal acknowledges sampling occurs but claims it doesn't affect representativeness, which is incorrect. If the model is trained/evaluated on resampled data, the AUPRC metric becomes invalid for real-world deployment where the natural, potentially shifting distribution occurs.

The rebuttal correctly addresses the failure mechanism. While the original finding argued that the GBM has an architectural advantage, the defender correctly notes that the experiment's goal is to isolate the value of event-level features, not to conduct a comprehensive model architecture comparison. The design gives both models equal preprocessing and tuning effort, making the comparison fair for its stated purpose. The performance gap between a well-tuned logistic regression on aggregates and a GBM on event-level features does meaningfully test whether the additional granularity provides value beyond what can be captured by linear models on aggregates. The concern about non-linearities in aggregates is valid but represents a different research question.

The rebuttal misapplies scope limitations. The methodology explicitly states the business objective is 'allocating limited intervention budget efficiently' and that 'precision ≥80% is enforced' due to budget constraints. The failure mechanism argues that AUPRC alone is insufficient for this specific business objective, and recall at the operational precision threshold is critical. This is not outside scope—it directly addresses whether the claimed performance improvement translates to the stated business objective. A model with higher AUPRC but lower recall at the required precision would be worse for deployment, making the primary metric misleading. The rebuttal's claim that this is 'not within the stated scope' ignores the methodology's own operational constraints.

The rebuttal correctly assesses this as immaterial to the core experiment claim. While excluding downgraders may affect the comprehensiveness of a production model, the experiment's goal is specifically to demonstrate predictive superiority of event-level features for predicting cancellations. The defender is correct that this exclusion is unlikely to invalidate the core finding about feature granularity, as the signal for cancellations should remain detectable even without downgraders. The concern about missing part of the risk spectrum is valid for deployment considerations but doesn't fundamentally undermine the experiment's internal validity for its stated scope.

defender_r2_output.pass_2_analysis: The critic has challenged several of my original rebuttals, raising valid concerns about temporal leakage, class distribution manipulation, and the relevance of the primary AUPRC metric to the stated business objective. I will need to strengthen my arguments on these points or concede the issues where the design does not adequately address the critic's concerns. However, the critic has accepted my rebuttals for F3 and F5, indicating those findings are not fatal to the experiment's conclusions.

rebuttals.justification: The critic is correct that the use of 'time elapsed since first play' calculated from the user's full history, even if consistent across splits, violates the strict temporal causality required for forward-looking deployment. In production, we would only know the elapsed time up to the current prediction time, not the full historical value. This represents a genuine flaw in the design that cannot be resolved by argument. While the intent was to use a user-level feature, the implementation fails to prevent this form of feature leakage from the future.

The critic is correct that my previous rebuttal contained a logical contradiction. If the class distribution is indeed balanced across splits via oversampling/undersampling, as the methodology states, then the natural distribution within each split is being artificially manipulated. This creates a mismatch between the evaluation data and the true production distribution, invalidating the AUPRC metric as a measure of real-world performance. I cannot defend this design choice, as the critic's challenge accurately identifies a flaw in the methodology.

The critic has accepted my original rebuttal, agreeing that the design provides a fair comparison between the gradient-boosted model and the logistic regression baseline by giving them equal preprocessing and tuning effort. The performance gap can therefore be attributed to the value of the event-level features, not architectural advantages.

The critic is correct that the stated business objective of 'allocating limited intervention budget efficiently' makes the recall at the 80% precision threshold a critical metric, not just AUPRC. While AUPRC may be a valid primary metric for demonstrating the predictive value of event-level features, the experiment must also show that this translates to improved performance at the specific operating point dictated by the business constraints. My previous scope-based rebuttal was too narrow and did not adequately address the relevance of the recall metric to the stated objective.

The critic has accepted my original rebuttal, agreeing that the exclusion of downgraders is unlikely to materially impact the core findings about the predictive value of event-level features. While this design choice may affect the comprehensiveness of a production model, it does not invalidate the experiment's ability to demonstrate the stated claim.

defender_r2_output.verdict_rationale: The critic has identified two genuine flaws in the design that cannot be resolved by argument: the temporal leakage in the 'time elapsed since first play' feature, and the artificial manipulation of class distributions across splits. These issues undermine the validity of the primary AUPRC metric and the ability to generalize the results to real-world deployment. While the design is sound in other areas, these critical flaws mean the critique must prevail.
